  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "What is stereotype threat in STEM? Should we address it? And how?",
  "opinion": "I've spent most of my life working as an electrician, primarily fixing wiring issues and ensuring that systems operate properly. Back when I was learning the trade, there weren't many books or courses available to me because I'm Muslim and from a lower secondary education background. Now, at seventy-three years old, I believe that everyone should be treated fairly regardless of their background.\n\nStereotype threat in STEM is like being afraid before you even start because people say electricians are not highly educated – but we do important work! In my field, it's similar to when a customer might doubt your abilities just because they don't see many with our kind of experience around. For example, I've seen young apprentices feeling intimidated by clients who think only university graduates can be good at this job.\n\nI feel strongly that if we're going to address stereotype threat in STEM, it's not about changing the actual work or how electricians operate – it's about making sure everyone knows you don't need a fancy degree to do skilled electrical work. We Muslims value knowledge and hard work above all else, so education should be accessible without feeling like you have to prove yourself against stereotypes.\n\nI think providing more hands-on training programs would help reduce this issue because I've learned through practical experience rather than just reading books. And maybe having mentors who come from various backgrounds could show young people that success is possible for everyone.",

response_parsed.topic: What is stereotype threat in STEM? Should we address it? And how?
response_parsed.opinion:
I've spent most of my life working as an electrician, primarily fixing wiring issues and ensuring that systems operate properly. Back when I was learning the trade, there weren't many books or courses available to me because I'm Muslim and from a lower secondary education background. Now, at seventy-three years old, I believe that everyone should be treated fairly regardless of their background.

Stereotype threat in STEM is like being afraid before you even start because people say electricians are not highly educated – but we do important work! In my field, it's similar to when a customer might doubt your abilities just because they don't see many with our kind of experience around. For example, I've seen young apprentices feeling intimidated by clients who think only university graduates can be good at this job.

I feel strongly that if we're going to address stereotype threat in STEM, it's not about changing the actual work or how electricians operate – it's about making sure everyone knows you don't need a fancy degree to do skilled electrical work. We Muslims value knowledge and hard work above all else, so education should be accessible without feeling like you have to prove yourself against stereotypes.

I think providing more hands-on training programs would help reduce this issue because I've learned through practical experience rather than just reading books. And maybe having mentors who come from various backgrounds could show young people that success is possible for everyone.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: From my background as an immigrant electrician with a lower secondary education, but raised by parents with diverse educational levels (one upper secondary, one master's), I've seen firsthand how stereotypes can limit opportunities. My low openness and neuroticism scores mean I prefer practical solutions over abstract debates. As someone who values hard work and is creative in problem-solving electrical issues, I believe addressing stereotype threat involves providing accessible hands-on training without relying on formal degrees.
response_parsed.tone: Reflective, down-to-earth, with a touch of wisdom gained from life experience.
